1|Abandon|To leave someone or something behind entirely.|The crew had to abandon the sinking ship to save themselves.|Desert, Leave, Dump
1|Baffle|To completely confuse or puzzle someone.|The complex math riddle will completely baffle anyone who tries it.|Confuse, Puzzle, Perplex
1|Cease|To bring or come to an end; stop.|The factory will cease production of the old toy next month.|Stop, Halt, End
1|Dawdle|To waste time or move very slowly.|If you dawdle on your way to school, you will be late.|Linger, Dally, Delay
1|Eager|Very keen or excited to do something.|The children were eager to open their presents on Christmas morning.|Keen, Enthusiastic, Anxious
1|Falter|To lose strength, momentum, or hesitate.|Her confidence began to falter when she forgot her lines on stage.|Hesitate, Stumble, Waver
1|Gather|To bring together into one group or place.|We need to gather wood before we can start the campfire.|Collect, Assemble, Accumulate
1|Habit|A regular practice or usual behavior.|Brushing your teeth twice a day is a very healthy habit.|Routine, Pattern, Custom
1|Ideal|Perfect, or the best possible standard.|A sunny day with a light breeze is ideal weather for a picnic.|Perfect, Flawless, Optimal
1|Jolly|Happy, cheerful, and full of fun.|Santa Claus is known for being a jolly man in a red suit.|Cheerful, Merry, Joyful
1|Keen|Having or showing eagerness or enthusiasm.|He is a keen reader who visits the library every weekend.|Eager, Enthusiastic, Sharp
1|Labor|Hard physical or mental work.|Building the Great Wall of China required years of intense labor.|Work, Toil, Effort
1|Mainly|For the most part; mostly.|The book is mainly about a boy who discovers a magical world.|Mostly, Primarily, Chiefly
1|Noble|Belonging to a high social class or having fine personal qualities.|The knight performed a noble deed by rescuing the trapped villagers.|Honorable, Virtuous, Aristocratic
1|Obey|To do what you are told to do.|You must obey the traffic lights to stay safe on the roads.|Comply, Follow, Submit
1|Pardon|To forgive or excuse someone for a mistake.|I beg your pardon, I didn't mean to bump into you.|Forgive, Excuse, Absolve
1|Quaint|Attractively unusual or old-fashioned.|They stayed in a quaint little cottage with a thatched roof.|Charming, Old-fashioned, Picturesque
1|Rare|Not occurring or found very often.|That blue diamond is incredibly rare and very valuable.|Uncommon, Scarce, Infrequent
1|Sacred|Connected with God or dedicated to a religious purpose.|The ancient temple is considered sacred by the local people.|Holy, Divine, Blessed
1|Tame|Not dangerous or frightened of people; domesticated.|The zoo keeper managed to tame the wild horse over several weeks.|Domesticated, Docile, Gentle
1|Upset|Unhappy, disappointed, or worried.|She was very upset when her favorite toy broke.|Distressed, Agitated, Troubled
1|Vague|Unclear, uncertain, or lacking detail.|His directions were so vague that we ended up getting lost.|Unclear, Indistinct, Hazy
1|Wander|To walk or move in a leisurely or aimless way.|We decided to wander through the forest and look at the trees.|Roam, Stroll, Meander
1|Yield|To produce or provide (an agricultural or industrial product).|The apple orchard will yield a huge crop this autumn.|Produce, Generate, Surrender
1|Zeal|Great energy or enthusiasm in pursuit of a cause or an objective.|He tackled the charity project with immense zeal.|Enthusiasm, Passion, Fervor
1|Abode|A place of residence; a house or home.|Welcome to my humble abode.|Home, Residence, Dwelling
1|Abstract|Existing in thought or as an idea.|Love is an abstract concept.|Theoretical, Conceptual
1|Abundance|A very large quantity of something.|There was an abundance of food at the feast.|Plenty, Wealth
1|Acclaim|Enthusiastic and public praise.|The new movie received critical acclaim.|Praise, Applause
1|Accolade|An award or privilege granted as a special honor.|He received the highest accolade for his bravery.|Award, Honor
1|Accommodate|To provide lodging or sufficient space for.|The hotel can accommodate up to 500 guests.|House, Shelter
1|Accompany|To go somewhere with someone as a companion.|I will accompany you to the train station.|Escort, Guide
1|Achieve|To successfully bring about or reach a desired objective.|She worked hard to achieve her goals.|Attain, Reach
1|Address|To speak to a person or group.|The mayor will address the crowd today.|Speak, Talk
1|Adhere|To stick fast to a surface or substance.|The glue will help the paper adhere to the wall.|Stick, Cling
1|Adjacent|Next to or adjoining something else.|Our house is adjacent to the local park.|Next, Bordering
1|Admission|A statement acknowledging the truth of something.|His admission of guilt shocked everyone.|Confession, Acceptance
1|Admonish|To warn or reprimand someone firmly.|The teacher had to admonish the noisy students.|Scold, Reprimand
1|Adopt|To choose to take up or follow an idea, method, or course of action.|The school will adopt a new uniform policy.|Embrace, Take on
1|Adverse|Preventing success or development; harmful.|The game was canceled due to adverse weather conditions.|Unfavorable, Bad
1|Aggressive|Ready or likely to attack or confront.|The aggressive dog growled at the strangers.|Hostile, Belligerent
1|Agitate|To make someone troubled or nervous.|The loud noises began to agitate the baby.|Upset, Disturb
1|Aid|Help, typically of a practical nature.|They gathered supplies to aid the flood victims.|Help, Assist
1|Albeit|Although.|He was making progress, albeit rather slowly.|Although, Even though
1|Allure|The quality of being powerfully and mysteriously attractive.|The allure of hidden treasure led them to the cave.|Attraction, Charm
1|Amateur|A person who engages in a pursuit on an unpaid basis.|The race was won by an unknown amateur.|Beginner, Nonprofessional
1|Ambrosial|Exceptionally pleasing to taste or smell.|The bakery was filled with ambrosial scents.|Delicious, Heavenly
1|Ample|Enough or more than enough; plentiful.|There is ample room for everyone in the hall.|Plentiful, Abundant
1|Antiquated|Old-fashioned or outdated.|They replaced the antiquated computers with modern ones.|Outdated, Obsolete
1|Apathy|Lack of interest, enthusiasm, or concern.|His apathy towards his homework resulted in bad grades.|Indifference, Unconcern
2|Abolish|To formally put an end to a system, practice, or institution.|The government decided to abolish the unfair tax laws.|Eliminate, Eradicate, Annul
2|Banish|To send someone away from a place as an official punishment.|The king chose to banish the traitor from the kingdom forever.|Exile, Expel, Deport
2|Calamity|An event causing great and often sudden damage or distress.|The earthquake was a terrible calamity for the small town.|Disaster, Catastrophe, Tragedy
2|Deceive|To deliberately cause someone to believe something that is not true.|The villain tried to deceive the hero with a clever disguise.|Trick, Mislead, Cheat
2|Earnest|Resulting from or showing sincere and intense conviction.|He made an earnest promise to always tell the truth.|Sincere, Serious, Solemn
2|Fatigue|Extreme tiredness resulting from mental or physical exertion.|After running the marathon, she collapsed from exhaustion and fatigue.|Exhaustion, Weariness, Tiredness
2|Grievance|A real or imagined wrong or other cause for complaint.|The workers filed a grievance about the unsafe conditions.|Complaint, Injustice, Grudge
2|Hoard|A hidden stock of valued goods or money.|The dragon slept on top of a massive hoard of gold.|Stash, Stockpile, Reserve
2|Ignoble|Not honorable in character or purpose.|Cheating on a test is an ignoble thing to do.|Dishonorable, Shameful, Base
2|Jubilant|Feeling or expressing great happiness and triumph.|The fans were jubilant when their team won the cup.|Overjoyed, Triumphant, Thrilled
2|Knack|An acquired or natural skill at performing a task.|She has a real knack for solving difficult puzzles quickly.|Skill, Talent, Flair
2|Lament|A passionate expression of grief or sorrow.|The poet wrote a sad lament for his lost friend.|Mourn, Grieve, Wail
2|Malice|The intention or desire to do evil or cause harm.|He broke her favorite vase out of pure malice.|Spite, Ill-will, Vengeance
2|Nimble|Quick and light in movement or action.|The nimble gymnast easily jumped over the high bar.|Agile, Quick, Spry
2|Obstinate|Stubbornly refusing to change one's opinion or chosen course of action.|The obstinate donkey refused to move an inch.|Stubborn, Inflexible, Headstrong
2|Peculiar|Strange or odd; unusual.|That animal has a peculiar habit of sleeping upside down.|Strange, Odd, Unusual
2|Quench|To satisfy one's thirst by drinking.|A glass of ice water will perfectly quench your thirst on a hot day.|Satisfy, Extinguish, Slake
2|Radiant|Sending out light; shining or glowing brightly.|The bride looked radiant in her beautiful white dress.|Shining, Glowing, Brilliant
2|Savage|Fierce, violent, and uncontrolled.|The explorer was attacked by a savage beast in the jungle.|Fierce, Wild, Untamed
2|Tact|Sensitivity in dealing with others or with difficult issues.|The teacher used great tact to explain the student's mistakes kindly.|Diplomacy, Sensitivity, Discretion
2|Unruly|Disorderly and disruptive and not amenable to discipline or control.|The unruly crowd pushed past the security guards.|Disorderly, Wild, Unmanageable
2|Valiant|Possessing or showing courage or determination.|The valiant knight fought bravely against the dragon.|Brave, Courageous, Heroic
2|Wary|Feeling or showing caution about possible dangers or problems.|You should be wary of strangers offering you free things.|Cautious, Careful, Suspicious
2|Yearn|Have an intense feeling of longing for something.|When she was away at camp, she began to yearn for her home.|Long, Desire, Crave
2|Zenith|The time at which something is most powerful or successful.|The Roman Empire peaked at its zenith before slowly declining.|Peak, Pinnacle, Apex
2|Appreciate|To recognize the full worth of.|I really appreciate all your help today.|Value, Treasure
2|Apprehensive|Anxious or fearful that something bad will happen.|She felt apprehensive about the upcoming exam.|Anxious, Nervous
2|Arduous|Involving or requiring strenuous effort; difficult and tiring.|Climbing the steep mountain was an arduous journey.|Difficult, Hard
2|Argument|An exchange of diverging or opposite views.|They had a heated argument about what game to play.|Dispute, Disagreement
2|Arid|Having little or no rain; too dry or barren.|Cacti thrive in the hot, arid desert.|Dry, Parched
2|Artful|Clever or skillful, typically in a crafty way.|The artful thief easily picked the lock.|Crafty, Sly
2|Articulate|Having the ability to speak fluently and coherently.|The articulate student gave a brilliant presentation.|Eloquent, Fluent
2|Artisan|A worker in a skilled trade, especially making things by hand.|The artisan crafted a beautiful wooden bowl.|Craftsman, Creator
2|Ashamed|Embarrassed or guilty because of one's actions.|He felt ashamed for breaking his brother's toy.|Sorry, Guilty
2|Assemble|To gather together in one place for a common purpose.|The students will assemble in the main hall.|Gather, Collect
2|Asunder|Apart; divided.|The mighty storm tore the old ship asunder.|Apart, Divided
2|Attempt|An effort to achieve or complete a difficult task.|She made a brave attempt to climb the wall.|Try, Effort
2|Augur|To portend a good or bad outcome.|The dark clouds augur a heavy thunderstorm.|Predict, Foretell
2|Austerity|Sternness or severity of manner or attitude.|The headmaster's austerity frightened the new pupils.|Severity, Strictness
2|Aversion|A strong dislike or disinclination.|He has a strong aversion to eating broccoli.|Dislike, Hatred
2|Avoid|To keep away from or stop oneself from doing.|You should avoid walking near the edge of the cliff.|Evade, Dodge
2|Avuncular|Kind and friendly toward a younger or less experienced person.|The avuncular old man gave the children sweets.|Friendly, Kind
2|Barbaric|Cruel and fiercely uncultured.|The gladiators fought in a barbaric and brutal manner.|Cruel, Savage
2|Because|For the reason that.|We stayed indoors because it was raining heavily.|Since, As
2|Beck|A gesture of the hand or head to encourage someone to approach.|The servant was at the king's beck and call.|Call, Command
2|Becoming|Flattering a person's appearance.|That blue dress is very becoming on you.|Flattering, Attractive
2|Bedfellow|A person connected with another in a particular activity.|Politics makes for strange bedfellows.|Associate, Partner
2|Bemoan|To express discontent or sorrow over.|He began to bemoan his lack of free time.|Lament, Mourn
2|Benefit|An advantage or profit gained from something.|Eating vegetables has a great benefit to your health.|Advantage, Good
2|Benign|Gentle and kindly.|The benign doctor smiled warmly at the patient.|Kind, Friendly
3|Abundant|Existing or available in large quantities; plentiful.|The farmer was pleased with the abundant harvest this year.|Plentiful, Copious, Ample
3|Benevolent|Well meaning and kindly.|The benevolent old man donated a lot of money to the local school.|Kind, Generous, Compassionate
3|Coax|To gently and persistently persuade someone to do something.|He tried to coax the frightened cat down from the tree with a treat.|Persuade, Wheedle, Cajole
3|Diligent|Having or showing care and conscientiousness in one's work.|She was a diligent student who always did her homework carefully.|Hardworking, Industrious, Careful
3|Elude|To evade or escape from a danger, enemy, or pursuer.|The clever thief managed to elude the police by hiding in an alley.|Evade, Escape, Dodge
3|Frugal|Sparing or economical with regard to money or food.|Because they were poor, they had to be frugal and save every penny.|Thrifty, Economical, Sparing
3|Gregarious|Fond of company; sociable.|Dolphins are gregarious animals that love to swim in large pods.|Sociable, Outgoing, Friendly
3|Haughty|Arrogantly superior and disdainful.|The haughty prince looked down on the poor villagers.|Arrogant, Proud, Snobbish
3|Impartial|Treating all rivals or disputants equally; fair and just.|A referee must remain impartial and not favor either team.|Fair, Neutral, Unbiased
3|Jeopardy|Danger of loss, harm, or failure.|His careless mistake put the entire mission in jeopardy.|Danger, Peril, Risk
3|Kindle|To light or set on fire; to arouse or inspire.|The teacher tried to kindle a love of reading in her students.|Ignite, Spark, Arouse
3|Lucid|Expressed clearly; easy to understand.|The instructions were so lucid that everyone knew exactly what to do.|Clear, Comprehensible, Plain
3|Meager|Lacking in quantity or quality.|The prisoners were given a meager portion of dry bread and water.|Scanty, Inadequate, Sparse
3|Novice|A person new to or inexperienced in a field or situation.|Since I am just a novice at chess, I easily lost to the champion.|Beginner, Learner, Rookie
3|Ominous|Giving the impression that something bad is going to happen.|The dark, ominous clouds meant a severe storm was coming.|Threatening, Menacing, Foreboding
3|Placid|Not easily upset or excited; calm and peaceful.|The placid lake perfectly reflected the surrounding mountains.|Calm, Peaceful, Tranquil
3|Queasy|Nauseated; feeling sick.|The bumpy rollercoaster ride made him feel quite queasy.|Nauseous, Sick, Squeamish
3|Reluctant|Unwilling and hesitant; disinclined.|She was reluctant to lend her favorite book to her brother.|Unwilling, Hesitant, Loath
3|Subtle|So delicate or precise as to be difficult to analyze or describe.|There was a subtle hint of garlic in the tomato sauce.|Faint, Delicate, Understated
3|Tranquil|Free from disturbance; calm.|The tranquil garden was the perfect place to sit and relax quietly.|Peaceful, Serene, Calm
3|Unique|Being the only one of its kind; unlike anything else.|Every snowflake is unique and has its own special pattern.|Distinctive, Singular, Exclusive
3|Vivid|Producing powerful feelings or strong, clear images in the mind.|She had a vivid dream that felt completely real.|Clear, Bright, Graphic
3|Wane|To decrease in vigor, power, or extent; become weaker.|As the night went on, his energy began to wane.|Diminish, Decline, Fade
3|Yoke|A wooden crosspiece fastened over the necks of two animals.|The farmer placed the yoke on the oxen so they could pull the cart.|Harness, Collar, Burden
3|Zest|Great enthusiasm and energy.|He approached every new challenge with boundless zest.|Enthusiasm, Vigor, Energy
3|Betray|To be disloyal to.|The spy decided to betray his own country.|Deceive, Cheat
3|Bewildered|Perplexed and confused; very puzzled.|The bewildered tourist asked for directions to the museum.|Confused, Puzzled
3|Biased|Unfairly prejudiced for or against someone or something.|A referee must never be biased during a match.|Prejudiced, Partial
3|Blissful|Extremely happy; full of joy.|They spent a blissful afternoon relaxing on the beach.|Joyful, Happy
3|Blunder|A stupid or careless mistake.|Sending the email to the wrong person was a huge blunder.|Mistake, Error
3|Bolster|To support or strengthen.|He drank coffee to bolster his energy levels.|Strengthen, Support
3|Bravado|A bold manner or a show of boldness intended to impress.|Behind his swaggering bravado, he was actually quite scared.|Boldness, Swagger
3|Brawn|Physical strength in contrast to intelligence.|The heavy lifting required more brawn than brain.|Strength, Muscle
3|Brusque|Abrupt or offhand in speech or manner.|The busy waiter gave a brusque reply and hurried away.|Abrupt, Blunt
3|Cache|A collection of items of the same type stored in a hidden place.|The pirates buried their cache of gold on the island.|Hoard, Stash
3|Candidate|A person who applies for a job or is nominated for election.|She was the best candidate for the manager role.|Applicant, Nominee
3|Career|An occupation undertaken for a significant period of a person's life.|He enjoyed a long and successful career as a doctor.|Profession, Job
3|Charismatic|Exercising a compelling charm that inspires devotion.|The charismatic leader easily won the crowd's support.|Charming, Magnetic
3|Charming|Pleasant or attractive.|They stayed in a charming little cottage in the woods.|Delightful, Appealing
3|Charred|Burned and blackened.|The toast was completely charred after being left in the oven.|Burned, Blackened
3|Coerce|To persuade an unwilling person to do something by using force.|The bully tried to coerce the boy into giving up his lunch.|Force, Compel
3|Coherent|Logical and consistent.|She presented a coherent and easy-to-understand argument.|Logical, Clear
3|Colloquial|Used in ordinary or familiar conversation; not formal.|'Gonna' and 'wanna' are colloquial expressions.|Informal, Conversational
3|Combination|A joining or merging of different parts.|The recipe uses a perfect combination of sweet and sour flavors.|Mixture, Blend
3|Comical|Amusing, especially in a ludicrous or absurd way.|The clown's oversized shoes looked highly comical.|Funny, Humorous
3|Commemorate|To recall and show respect for someone or something.|The statue was built to commemorate the brave soldiers.|Remember, Honor
3|Compel|To force or oblige someone to do something.|The strong evidence will compel the jury to find him guilty.|Force, Coerce
3|Complex|Consisting of many different and connected parts.|The human brain is an incredibly complex organ.|Complicated, Intricate
3|Compliance|The action of complying with a wish or command.|Strict compliance with the safety rules is mandatory.|Obedience, Agreement
3|Comrade|A companion who shares one's activities or is a fellow member.|The soldier refused to leave his injured comrade behind.|Companion, Friend
4|Accomplice|A person who helps another commit a crime.|The thief was caught, but his accomplice managed to run away.|Partner, Associate, Collaborator
4|Boisterous|Noisy, energetic, and cheerful; rowdy.|The boisterous children ran around the playground shouting loudly.|Noisy, Rowdy, Energetic
4|Conceal|To keep from sight; hide.|He tried to conceal the surprise present behind his back.|Hide, Cover, Mask
4|Deplete|To use up the supply or resources of.|Long droughts severely deplete the water supply in the reservoir.|Exhaust, Drain, Consume
4|Enigma|A person or thing that is mysterious, puzzling, or difficult to understand.|How the ancient pyramids were built remains a fascinating enigma.|Mystery, Puzzle, Riddle
4|Fictitious|Not real or true, being imaginary or having been fabricated.|The characters in the story are completely fictitious and not based on real people.|Imaginary, Made-up, False
4|Grueling|Extremely tiring and demanding.|The marathon is a grueling race that tests a runner's endurance.|Exhausting, Punishing, Demanding
4|Hostile|Unfriendly; antagonistic.|The stray dog gave a hostile growl when I approached.|Unfriendly, Aggressive, Belligerent
4|Inevitable|Certain to happen; unavoidable.|Since they never practiced, their defeat in the final was inevitable.|Unavoidable, Inescapable, Certain
4|Jovial|Cheerful and friendly.|The jovial host greeted every guest with a wide smile and a warm hug.|Cheerful, Jolly, Merry
4|Kinship|Blood relationship; a sharing of characteristics or origins.|They felt a strong kinship because they shared the same unusual hobby.|Affinity, Connection, Relation
4|Linger|To stay in a place longer than necessary.|She decided to linger in the park to watch the sunset.|Loiter, Dawdle, Stay
4|Momentous|Of great importance or significance.|The signing of the peace treaty was a momentous occasion for the country.|Significant, Historic, Crucial
4|Nostalgia|A sentimental longing or wistful affection for the past.|Looking at old family photos always fills him with a sense of nostalgia.|Reminiscence, Longing, Homesickness
4|Obsolete|No longer produced or used; out of date.|Typewriters became obsolete once computers were invented.|Outdated, Antiquated, Extinct
4|Peril|Serious and immediate danger.|The sailors were in great peril when the fierce storm hit their ship.|Danger, Hazard, Risk
4|Quiver|To tremble or shake with a slight rapid motion.|Her lip began to quiver when she realized she had lost her ticket.|Tremble, Shake, Shiver
4|Replenish|To fill something up again.|The hikers stopped at the stream to replenish their water bottles.|Refill, Restock, Restore
4|Somber|Dark or dull in color or tone; gloomy.|Everyone at the funeral wore dark, somber clothing.|Gloomy, Solemn, Serious
4|Trivial|Of little value or importance.|They wasted hours arguing over trivial details instead of doing the work.|Unimportant, Insignificant, Minor
4|Unanimous|Fully in agreement.|The jury reached a unanimous decision that the man was innocent.|United, Agreed, Undivided
4|Versatile|Able to adapt or be adapted to many different functions or activities.|A Swiss Army knife is a highly versatile tool.|Adaptable, Flexible, Multipurpose
4|Whimsical|Playfully quaint or fanciful, especially in an appealing and amusing way.|The artist painted a whimsical picture of flying pigs and purple trees.|Fanciful, Playful, Quirky
4|Yielding|Complying with the requests or desires of others.|The yielding manager agreed to give his staff the afternoon off.|Compliant, Accommodating, Submissive
4|Zephyr|A soft gentle breeze.|A warm summer zephyr gently rustled the leaves of the trees.|Breeze, Draft, Wind
4|Conceited|Excessively proud of oneself; vain.|The conceited actor couldn't stop looking in the mirror.|Vain, Proud
4|Conclusion|The end or finish of an event or process.|The exciting movie reached a dramatic conclusion.|End, Finish
4|Conclusive|Serving to prove a case; decisive.|The fingerprint was conclusive proof that he was there.|Decisive, Definite
4|Concur|To be of the same opinion; agree.|The doctors concur that she needs plenty of rest.|Agree, Assent
4|Condemn|To express complete disapproval of, typically in public.|The community will condemn the cruel actions of the criminal.|Criticize, Blame
4|Condone|To accept and allow wrong behavior to continue.|The school cannot condone bullying of any kind.|Allow, Overlook
4|Conducive|Making a certain situation or outcome likely or possible.|A quiet room is conducive to studying effectively.|Favorable, Helpful
4|Confiscated|Taken or seized with authority.|The teacher confiscated the mobile phone during the lesson.|Seized, Taken
4|Conjecture|An opinion or conclusion formed on the basis of incomplete information.|Without facts, your theory is pure conjecture.|Guess, Speculation
4|Consent|Permission for something to happen or agreement to do something.|You must get your parents' consent to go on the trip.|Permission, Agreement
4|Consequently|As a result.|He didn't study, and consequently failed the test.|Therefore, Thus
4|Consideration|Careful thought, typically over a period of time.|After much consideration, she chose the blue car.|Thought, Deliberation
4|Conspire|To make secret plans jointly to commit an unlawful act.|The villains conspire to steal the royal crown.|Plot, Scheme
4|Consume|To eat, drink, or ingest.|A growing lion will consume a large amount of meat.|Eat, Devour
4|Contagious|Spread from one person or organism to another by contact.|A common cold is a highly contagious illness.|Infectious, Catching
4|Contemporary|Living or occurring at the same time.|The museum features a stunning collection of contemporary art.|Modern, Current
4|Contrast|The state of being strikingly different from something else.|The white snow provided a sharp contrast to the dark trees.|Difference, Disparity
4|Contrite|Feeling or expressing remorse or penitence.|The contrite child apologized for breaking the window.|Sorry, Remorseful
4|Conundrum|A confusing and difficult problem or question.|Figuring out the riddle proved to be a real conundrum.|Puzzle, Mystery
4|Convict|To declare someone to be guilty of a criminal offense.|The judge will convict the thief based on the evidence.|Sentence, Condemn
4|Cordial|Warm and friendly.|The host gave us a very cordial welcome to the party.|Friendly, Warm
4|Covet|To yearn to possess or have something.|I secretly covet my best friend's shiny new bicycle.|Desire, Crave
4|Coy|Making a pretense of shyness or modesty that is intended to be alluring.|She gave him a coy smile from across the room.|Shy, Bashful
4|Crass|Lacking sensitivity, refinement, or intelligence.|His crass jokes offended everyone at the formal dinner.|Insensitive, Vulgar
4|Credit|The ability of a customer to obtain goods or services before payment.|He bought the expensive television on credit.|Trust, Loan
5|Adamant|Refusing to be persuaded or to change one's mind.|He was adamant that he had not eaten the last cookie.|Resolute, Unshakable, Stubborn
5|Blemish|A small mark or flaw which spoils the appearance of something.|There was a tiny blemish on the skin of the perfect red apple.|Flaw, Defect, Spot
5|Candid|Truthful and straightforward; frank.|I want you to be candid and tell me honestly what you think of my painting.|Frank, Honest, Outspoken
5|Desolate|Deserted of people and in a state of bleak and dismal emptiness.|After the town was abandoned, it became a desolate wasteland.|Barren, Empty, Bleak
5|Eccentric|Unconventional and slightly strange.|The eccentric inventor wore mismatched shoes and talked to himself.|Unconventional, Odd, Quirky
5|Flaw|A mark, fault, or other imperfection.|The only flaw in his plan was that he forgot to bring a map.|Defect, Blemish, Fault
5|Gullible|Easily persuaded to believe something; credulous.|My gullible brother believed me when I told him the moon was made of cheese.|Credulous, Naive, Trusting
5|Hinder|To create difficulties for someone or something, resulting in delay.|The heavy snow will hinder the rescue team's progress.|Impede, Obstruct, Delay
5|Illustrious|Well known, respected, and admired for past achievements.|The illustrious scientist was awarded a Nobel Prize for her discoveries.|Eminent, Distinguished, Renowned
5|Jubilation|A feeling of great happiness and triumph.|There was widespread jubilation when the war finally ended.|Joy, Exultation, Triumph
5|Luminous|Full of or shedding light; bright or shining.|The moon cast a luminous glow over the dark ocean.|Radiant, Glowing, Brilliant
5|Meticulous|Showing great attention to detail; very careful and precise.|She is a meticulous cleaner who leaves no corner un-dusted.|Careful, Exact, Thorough
5|Notorious|Famous or well known, typically for some bad quality or deed.|The notorious pirate Blackbeard was feared across the seven seas.|Infamous, Scandalous, Disreputable
5|Oppressive|Unjustly inflicting hardship and constraint.|The dictator ruled his country with an oppressive and cruel regime.|Harsh, Cruel, Tyrannical
5|Pristine|In its original condition; unspoiled.|The explorer was the first to set foot on the pristine, untouched island.|Immaculate, Spotless, Pure
5|Quandary|A state of perplexity or uncertainty over what to do in a difficult situation.|I am in a quandary about whether to go to the party or stay home and study.|Dilemma, Predicament, Fix
5|Rigorous|Extremely thorough, exhaustive, or accurate.|The athletes underwent a rigorous training program to prepare for the Olympics.|Strict, Severe, Thorough
5|Scrutinize|To examine or inspect closely and thoroughly.|The detective had to scrutinize the crime scene for tiny clues.|Examine, Inspect, Study
5|Tenacious|Tending to keep a firm hold of something; clinging or adhering closely.|The tenacious climber refused to let go of the rock, despite his exhaustion.|Firm, Persistent, Resolute
5|Unprecedented|Never done or known before.|The internet has allowed information to spread at an unprecedented speed.|Unparalleled, Unknown, Unmatched
5|Vigilant|Keeping careful watch for possible danger or difficulties.|The security guard must remain vigilant throughout the night.|Watchful, Observant, Alert
5|Wrath|Extreme anger.|He hid from the dragon to avoid its fiery wrath.|Anger, Rage, Fury
5|Yearning|A feeling of intense longing for something.|She felt a deep yearning to travel and see the world.|Longing, Craving, Desire
5|Appetite|A natural desire to satisfy a bodily need, especially for food.|Swimming in the cold ocean gave him a huge appetite.|Hunger, Craving, Taste
5|Belligerent|Hostile and aggressive.|The belligerent customer yelled loudly at the poor cashier.|Hostile, Aggressive, Pugnacious
5|Cunning|Having or showing skill in achieving one's ends by deceit or evasion.|The cunning fox managed to steal the farmer's chickens.|Sly, Crafty
5|Curb|To restrain or keep in check.|You must learn to curb your anger when things go wrong.|Restrain, Control
5|Curious|Eager to know or learn something.|The curious kitten sniffed at the strange new toy.|Inquisitive, Intrigued
5|Curt|Rudely brief.|His curt reply showed that he was annoyed by the question.|Abrupt, Blunt
5|Dazzling|Extremely bright, especially so as to blind the eyes temporarily.|She wore a dazzling diamond necklace to the ball.|Brilliant, Shining
5|Debate|A formal discussion on a particular topic.|The politicians held a lively debate on live television.|Discussion, Argument
5|Decline|To politely refuse an invitation or offer.|I must decline your offer because I am too busy.|Refuse, Reject
5|Definite|Clearly stated or decided; not vague or doubtful.|We need a definite answer by tomorrow morning.|Clear, Certain
5|Defy|To openly resist or refuse to obey.|The brave rebel dared to defy the cruel king's orders.|Resist, Oppose
5|Deject|To make sad or dispirited; depress.|Failing the important exam will deeply deject him.|Depress, Sadden
5|Deluge|A severe flood.|A sudden deluge of rain flooded the streets in minutes.|Flood, Torrent
5|Demolish|To pull or knock down a building.|The builders will demolish the old factory tomorrow.|Destroy, Wreck
5|Demure|Reserved, modest, and shy.|The demure young lady sat quietly in the corner.|Modest, Shy
5|Depict|To show or represent by a drawing, painting, or other art form.|The cave paintings depict ancient hunting scenes.|Draw, Portray
5|Desist|To cease; abstain.|The police officer ordered the crowd to desist from shouting.|Stop, Cease
5|Destitute|Without the basic necessities of life.|The terrible fire left the poor family completely destitute.|Poor, Penniless
5|Develop|To grow or cause to grow and become more mature, advanced, or elaborate.|A small seed will develop into a massive oak tree.|Grow, Evolve
5|Devise|To plan or invent a complex procedure, system, or mechanism.|They need to devise a clever plan to escape the maze.|Invent, Plan
5|Devour|To eat food hungrarily or quickly.|The hungry wolves will quickly devour their prey.|Eat, Consume
5|Diction|The choice and use of words and phrases in speech or writing.|The poet's clear diction made the poem easy to understand.|Phrasing, Wording
5|Digest|To break down food in the stomach.|A snake can take days to fully digest a large meal.|Absorb, Process
5|Digress|To leave the main subject temporarily in speech or writing.|I have a tendency to digress when I tell long stories.|Wander, Stray
5|Diluted|Made thinner or weaker by adding water or another solvent.|The orange squash was too strong, so he diluted it with water.|Watered-down, Weakened
5|Dim|Not shining brightly or clearly.|The dim candlelight made it hard to read the book.|Faint, Weak
5|Diminish|To make or become less.|The pain in his leg began to diminish after taking medicine.|Decrease, Reduce
6|Amiable|Having or displaying a friendly and pleasant manner.|The amiable shopkeeper always greeted her customers with a big smile.|Friendly, Affable, Pleasant
6|Brevity|Concise and exact use of words in writing or speech.|Because we only have five minutes, the brevity of your speech is important.|Conciseness, Shortness, Terseness
6|Callous|Showing or having an insensitive and cruel disregard for others.|The callous thief stole money from the charity box without feeling guilty.|Heartless, Insensitive, Cruel
6|Daunting|Seeming difficult to deal with in anticipation; intimidating.|Climbing Mount Everest is a daunting task for even the best mountaineers.|Intimidating, Formidable, Overwhelming
6|Eloquent|Fluent or persuasive in speaking or writing.|She gave an eloquent speech that moved everyone in the audience to tears.|Expressive, Articulate, Fluent
6|Frivolous|Not having any serious purpose or value.|He spent his entire allowance on frivolous things like arcade games and candy.|Flippant, Senseless, Trivial
6|Garrulous|Excessively talkative, especially on trivial matters.|The garrulous passenger next to me on the plane wouldn't stop chatting.|Talkative, Loquacious, Voluble

6|Hypocrisy|The practice of claiming to have moral standards to which one's own behavior does not conform.|It is pure hypocrisy for him to tell us to save the environment while he drops litter.|Deceit, Two-facedness, Insincerity
6|Impeccable|In accordance with the highest standards; faultless.|The butler's manners were absolutely impeccable.|Flawless, Perfect, Spotless
6|Judicious|Having, showing, or done with good judgment or sense.|It is judicious to save some of your money instead of spending it all at once.|Wise, Prudent, Sensible
6|Lethargic|Affected by lethargy; sluggish and apathetic.|The hot, humid weather made everyone feel tired and lethargic.|Sluggish, Inert, Inactive
6|Morose|Sullen and ill-tempered.|After losing the match, he sat in the corner looking gloomy and morose.|Sullen, Sulky, Gloomy
6|Nuance|A subtle difference in or shade of meaning, expression, or sound.|The actor's performance captured every tiny nuance of the character's sadness.|Subtlety, Degree, Variation
6|Ostentatious|Characterized by vulgar or pretentious display.|He drove an ostentatious gold car to show off his wealth.|Showy, Pretentious, Flamboyant
6|Prolific|Present in large numbers or quantities; plentiful.|Shakespeare was a prolific writer who produced dozens of plays and poems.|Productive, Abundant, Fruitful
6|Quagmire|An awkward, complex, or hazardous situation.|The project became a quagmire of rules and regulations that slowed us down.|Muddle, Predicament, Tangle
6|Resilient|Able to withstand or recover quickly from difficult conditions.|Children are often incredibly resilient and bounce back quickly from illnesses.|Tough, Hardy, Buoyant
6|Sporadic|Occurring at irregular intervals or only in a few places.|We heard sporadic gunfire echoing from the distant hills.|Occasional, Infrequent, Irregular
6|Trepidation|A feeling of fear or agitation about something that may happen.|She approached the haunted house with great trepidation.|Fear, Apprehension, Dread
6|Ubiquitous|Present, appearing, or found everywhere.|Mobile phones have become completely ubiquitous in modern society.|Omnipresent, Universal, Pervasive
6|Voracious|Wanting or devouring great quantities of food or information.|He is a voracious reader who gets through three books a week.|Insatiable, Unquenchable, Greedy
6|Wane|To decrease in power or size.|As the storm passed, the fierce winds began to wane.|Diminish, Decline, Fade
6|Abstain|To restrain oneself from doing or enjoying something.|During his diet, he had to abstain from eating chocolate.|Refrain, Desist, Forbear
6|Brazen|Bold and without shame.|The brazen thief walked right into the bank without wearing a mask.|Bold, Shameless, Audacious
6|Clandestine|Kept secret or done secretively, especially because illicit.|The spies held a clandestine meeting in the dark basement.|Secret, Covert, Stealthy
6|Discredit|To harm the good reputation of someone or something.|The newspaper tried to discredit the honest mayor.|Defame, Slander
6|Dishevelled|Untidy; disordered.|He woke up looking totally dishevelled with messy hair.|Untidy, Messy
6|Disinterested|Not influenced by considerations of personal advantage.|A judge must be completely disinterested when hearing a case.|Unbiased, Impartial
6|Dismal|Depressing; dreary.|The dismal grey weather ruined our trip to the seaside.|Gloomy, Dreary
6|Disperse|To distribute or spread over a wide area.|The wind will disperse the dandelion seeds across the field.|Scatter, Spread
6|Divine|Of, from, or like God or a god.|The ancient Greeks believed lightning was a sign of divine anger.|Holy, Heavenly
6|Docile|Ready to accept control or instruction; submissive.|The normally fierce dog was surprisingly docile with the baby.|Obedient, Tame
6|Dogmatic|Inclined to lay down principles as incontrovertibly true.|The dogmatic teacher refused to listen to other opinions.|Strict, Rigid
6|Domestic|Relating to the running of a home or to family relations.|Washing dishes is a necessary domestic chore.|Household, Family
6|Dormant|Having normal physical functions suspended or slowed down for a period of time.|The dormant volcano hasn't erupted in hundreds of years.|Sleeping, Inactive
6|Dose|A quantity of a medicine or drug taken at a particular time.|The doctor prescribed a strong dose of cough syrup.|Amount, Portion
6|Doubtful|Feeling uncertain about something.|I am highly doubtful that it will snow in the middle of summer.|Uncertain, Unsure
6|Dour|Relentlessly severe, stern, or gloomy in manner or appearance.|The dour security guard never smiled at anyone.|Stern, Severe
6|Draconian|Excessively harsh and severe.|The king imposed draconian punishments for even minor crimes.|Harsh, Severe
6|Dutiful|Conscientiously or obediently fulfilling one's duty.|The dutiful knight followed his king's orders perfectly.|Obedient, Loyal
6|Egoistical|Excessively conceited or absorbed in oneself; self-centered.|The egoistical singer only talked about her own talent.|Selfish, Vain
6|Elementary|Relating to the most rudimentary aspects of a subject.|Adding two plus two is an elementary math problem.|Basic, Simple
6|Eloquence|Fluent or persuasive speaking or writing.|His natural eloquence made him a fantastic debater.|Fluency, Articulacy
6|Elucidate|To make something clear; explain.|Please elucidate your theory so the class can understand.|Explain, Clarify
6|Embargo|An official ban on trade or other commercial activity with a particular country.|The country placed a strict embargo on importing foreign cars.|Ban, Restriction
6|Embrace|To hold someone closely in one's arms, especially as a sign of affection.|The mother ran to embrace her returning son.|Hug, Hold
6|Empire|An extensive group of states or countries under a single supreme authority.|The Roman Empire stretched across much of Europe.|Kingdom, Realm
6|Emulate|To match or surpass a person or achievement, typically by imitation.|The young boy tried to emulate his older brother's success.|Imitate, Copy
6|Endorse|To declare one's public approval or support of.|The famous athlete was paid to endorse the new sports shoe.|Support, Approve
6|Endure|To suffer something painful or difficult patiently.|The explorers had to endure freezing temperatures in the Arctic.|Suffer, Withstand
7|Alleviate|To make suffering, deficiency, or a problem less severe.|Taking this medicine should help alleviate your headache.|Relieve, Ease, Lessen
7|Banal|So lacking in originality as to be obvious and boring.|The movie's plot was so banal that I could guess the ending in five minutes.|Trite, Cliched, Ordinary
7|Capricious|Given to sudden and unaccountable changes of mood or behavior.|The capricious weather went from sunny to hailing in just ten minutes.|Fickle, Inconstant, Unpredictable
7|Derive|To obtain something from a specified source.|Many medicines we use today derive from plants found in the rainforest.|Obtain, Get, Acquire
7|Enthrall|To capture the fascinated attention of.|The magician's incredible tricks managed to enthrall the entire audience.|Captivate, Charm, Fascinate
7|Fastidious|Very attentive to and concerned about accuracy and detail.|The fastidious artist spent hours perfecting the smallest stroke of paint.|Meticulous, Scrupulous, Punctilious
7|Gratuitous|Uncalled for; lacking good reason; unwarranted.|The extra violence in the movie was completely gratuitous and unnecessary.|Unjustified, Unprovoked, Needless
7|Impetuous|Acting or done quickly and without thought or care.|His impetuous decision to quit his job meant he had no money for rent.|Impulsive, Rash, Hasty
7|Juxtapose|To place or deal with close together for contrasting effect.|The museum decided to juxtapose modern art right next to ancient artifacts.|Compare, Contrast, Collocate
7|Loquacious|Tending to talk a great deal; talkative.|The loquacious tour guide talked continuously for the entire two-hour trip.|Talkative, Voluble, Garrulous
7|Magnanimous|Very generous or forgiving, especially toward a rival.|In a magnanimous gesture, the winner praised the runner-up's incredible skill.|Generous, Charitable, Forgiving
7|Nefarious|Wicked or criminal (typically of an action or activity).|The nefarious villain plotted to steal the city's entire gold reserve.|Wicked, Evil, Villainous
7|Obfuscate|To render obscure, unclear, or unintelligible.|The politician tried to obfuscate the truth by using complicated, confusing words.|Obscure, Confuse, Blur
7|Paradigm|A typical example or pattern of something; a model.|Her successful business model became the new paradigm for the entire industry.|Model, Pattern, Example
7|Quintessential|Representing the most perfect or typical example of a quality or class.|The cozy cottage with a roaring fire was the quintessential country home.|Typical, Classic, Ideal
7|Reticent|Not revealing one's thoughts or feelings readily.|He was reticent about his past and rarely spoke about his family.|Reserved, Withdrawn, Introverted
7|Sycophant|A person who acts obsequiously toward someone important to gain advantage.|The sycophant constantly complimented the king just to get rich rewards.|Toady, Flatterer, Yes-man
7|Taciturn|Reserved or uncommunicative in speech; saying little.|The taciturn old cowboy simply nodded instead of answering my question.|Untalkative, Quiet, Mute
7|Vacillate|To alternate or waver between different opinions or actions; be indecisive.|If you vacillate too long on the menu, the restaurant will close.|Waver, Hesitate, Dither
7|Whimsical|Playfully quaint or fanciful.|Her whimsical clothing style included colorful striped socks and top hats.|Fanciful, Playful, Quirky
7|Aberration|A departure from what is normal, usual, or expected.|A snowstorm in the middle of July is a complete aberration.|Anomaly, Deviation, Abnormality
7|Cacophony|A harsh, discordant mixture of sounds.|The loud construction work created a terrible cacophony that gave me a headache.|Din, Racket, Noise
7|Dearth|A scarcity or lack of something.|There is a concerning dearth of fresh water in the desert region.|Lack, Scarcity, Shortage
7|Ebullient|Cheerful and full of energy.|She felt highly ebullient after finding out she got full marks on the test.|Exuberant, Buoyant, Joyful
7|Facetious|Treating serious issues with deliberately inappropriate humor.|Please stop being facetious when we are discussing safety rules.|Flippant, Glued, Frivolous
7|Enterprise|A project or undertaking, typically one that is difficult or requires effort.|Building a colony on Mars is a massive enterprise.|Venture, Project
7|Enthuse|To say something that expresses one's eager enjoyment, interest, or approval.|She began to enthuse over her brilliant exam results.|Rave, Gush
7|Entire|With no part left out; whole.|He was so hungry he ate the entire pizza by himself.|Whole, Complete
7|Equivocate|To use ambiguous language so as to conceal the truth or avoid committing oneself.|When asked directly, the politician began to equivocate.|Prevaricate, Dodge
7|Era|A long and distinct period of history with a particular feature or characteristic.|The Victorian era brought huge changes to industry.|Period, Age
7|Erect|Rigidly upright or straight.|The soldiers stood erect as the general walked past.|Upright, Straight
7|Erratic|Not even or regular in pattern or movement; unpredictable.|The bat flew in an erratic, zigzag pattern across the room.|Unpredictable, Irregular
7|Especially|Used to single out one person, thing, or situation over all others.|I love all chocolate, especially the dark kind.|Particularly, Specifically
7|Etiquette|The customary code of polite behavior in society.|It is bad etiquette to chew with your mouth open.|Manners, Protocol
7|Exaggerate|To represent something as being larger, better, or worse than it really is.|He tends to exaggerate how big the fish he caught was.|Overstate, Embellish
7|Exclaim|To cry out suddenly, especially in surprise, anger, or pain.|She couldn't help but exclaim in joy when she won the prize.|Cry out, Shout
7|Fact|A thing that is indisputably the case.|It is a scientific fact that the Earth revolves around the sun.|Truth, Reality
7|Famine|Extreme scarcity of food.|The long drought caused a terrible famine across the region.|Starvation, Scarcity
7|Fatal|Causing death.|The brave knight struck a fatal blow to the fearsome dragon.|Deadly, Lethal
7|Faux|Made in imitation; artificial.|She wore a glamorous faux fur coat to the party.|Fake, Artificial
7|Feeble|Lacking physical strength, especially as a result of age or illness.|The old dog let out a weak and feeble bark.|Weak, Frail
7|Feign|To pretend to be affected by a feeling, state, or injury.|The smart player will feign an injury to stop the clock.|Pretend, Fake
7|Fiasco|A thing that is a complete failure, especially in a ludicrous or humiliating way.|The poorly planned party turned into a total fiasco.|Disaster, Catastrophe
7|Figment|A thing that someone believes to be real but that exists only in their imagination.|The monster under the bed is just a figment of your imagination.|Illusion, Creation
7|Figurative|Departing from a literal use of words; metaphorical.|'Raining cats and dogs' is a figurative expression.|Metaphorical, Symbolic
7|Flamboyant|Tending to attract attention because of their exuberance, confidence, and stylishness.|The flamboyant singer wore a glittery jacket and bright pink sunglasses.|Showy, Ostentatious
7|Flattering|Full of praise and compliments.|He made several flattering remarks about her beautiful painting.|Complimentary, Praising
7|Flawed|Blemished, damaged, or imperfect in some way.|The detective realized the witness's flawed story didn't make sense.|Imperfect, Defective
7|Flawless|Without any blemishes or imperfections; perfect.|The gymnast performed a truly flawless routine on the bars.|Perfect, Unblemished
7|Flee|To run away from a place or situation of danger.|The villagers had to flee when the volcano began to erupt.|Escape, Run
8|Acquiesce|To accept something reluctantly but without protest.|Though he didn't want to go to the ballet, he decided to acquiesce to make her happy.|Consent, Comply, Yield

8|Bourgeois|Of or characteristic of the middle class, typically with reference to its perceived materialistic values.|They lived a comfortable, bourgeois lifestyle in a quiet suburban neighborhood.|Middle-class, Conventional, Materialistic
8|Chicanery|The use of trickery to achieve a political, financial, or legal purpose.|The corrupt politician was found guilty of financial chicanery.|Trickery, Deception, Subterfuge
8|Deleterious|Causing harm or damage.|Smoking cigarettes has a highly deleterious effect on your lungs.|Harmful, Damaging, Detrimental
8|Epiphany|A moment of sudden revelation or insight.|While washing the dishes, he had an epiphany about how to solve the difficult maths equation.|Revelation, Insight, Realization
8|Facilitate|To make an action or process easy or easier.|The new ramp will facilitate wheelchair access to the building.|Ease, Assist, Help
8|Galvanize|To shock or excite someone into taking action.|The inspiring speech helped to galvanize the crowd into protesting the new law.|Spur, Motivate, Stimulate
8|Harangue|A lengthy and aggressive speech.|The angry headmaster gave a terrifying harangue about school behavior.|Tirade, Diatribe, Rant
8|Iconoclast|A person who attacks cherished beliefs or institutions.|The daring artist was an iconoclast who mocked traditional painting rules.|Critic, Skeptic, Maverick
8|Jejune|Naive, simplistic, and superficial.|His jejune remarks showed that he didn't really understand the serious topic.|Naive, Simple, Childish
8|Kowtow|To act in an excessively subservient manner.|The employees had to kowtow to the demanding boss out of fear of losing their jobs.|Grovel, Pander, Fawn
8|Laissez-faire|A policy or attitude of letting things take their own course.|The government took a laissez-faire approach and let businesses regulate themselves.|Non-interference, Hands-off, Relaxed
8|Mendacious|Not telling the truth; lying.|The mendacious witness gave completely false evidence in court.|Lying, Untruthful, False
8|Nonchalant|Feeling or appearing casually calm and relaxed.|Despite the extreme danger, the spy remained cool and nonchalant.|Calm, Cool, Unconcerned
8|Omnipotent|Having unlimited power; able to do anything.|In Greek mythology, Zeus is considered an almost omnipotent god.|All-powerful, Supreme, Invincible
8|Panacea|A solution or remedy for all difficulties or diseases.|Unfortunately, there is no magical panacea to instantly cure poverty.|Cure-all, Elixir, Magic bullet
8|Quotidian|Of or occurring every day; daily.|Washing the dishes is a boring but necessary quotidian task.|Daily, Everyday, Ordinary
8|Recalcitrant|Having an obstinately uncooperative attitude toward authority.|The recalcitrant teenager refused to listen to his parents or his teachers.|Uncooperative, Rebellious, Defiant
8|Serendipity|The occurrence and development of events by chance in a happy or beneficial way.|By pure serendipity, I bumped into my best friend while on holiday in Spain.|Chance, Luck, Fluke
8|Tantamount|Equivalent in seriousness to; virtually the same as.|Leaving a dog in a hot car is tantamount to animal cruelty.|Equivalent, Equal, Synonymous
8|Usurp|To take a position of power or importance illegally or by force.|The evil brother tried to usurp the throne while the king was ill.|Seize, Take over, Overthrow
8|Vehement|Showing strong feeling; forceful, passionate, or intense.|She made a vehement denial when accused of cheating.|Passionate, Forceful, Intense
8|Abstemious|Not self-indulgent, especially when eating and drinking.|The monks lived an abstemious life, eating only plain rice and vegetables.|Temperate, Restrained, Moderate

8|Bowdlerize|To remove material material that is considered improper or offensive from a text.|The publisher decided to bowdlerize the classic novel to make it suitable for young children.|Censor, Expurgate, Purify
8|Circumlocution|The use of many words where fewer would do.|Instead of just saying 'no', the politician used endless circumlocution to dodge the question.|Wordiness, Verbosity, Waffle
8|Fleet|Fast and nimble in movement.|The fleet runner easily won the 100-meter dash.|Fast, Quick
8|Flimsy|Comparatively light and insubstantial; easily damaged.|The flimsy paper umbrella broke in the heavy wind.|Fragile, Weak
8|Flippant|Not showing a serious or respectful attitude.|His flippant remark during the serious assembly got him in trouble.|Frivolous, Disrespectful
8|Flora|The plants of a particular region, habitat, or geological period.|The botanist studied the unique flora of the Amazon rainforest.|Plants, Vegetation
8|Flourish|To grow or develop in a healthy or vigorous way.|The plants will flourish if they get enough sunlight and water.|Thrive, Grow
8|Foe|An enemy or opponent.|In the story, the brave hero faces a powerful and evil foe.|Enemy, Adversary
8|Forebear|An ancestor.|Our ancient forebear painted these amazing pictures on the cave walls.|Ancestor, Forefather
8|Forlorn|Pitifully sad and abandoned or lonely.|The forlorn puppy waited in the rain for its owner to return.|Sad, Miserable
8|Foundation|The lowest load-bearing part of a building, typically below ground level.|The builders poured concrete to create a strong foundation for the house.|Base, Bottom
8|Fragment|A small part broken or separated off something.|She swept up every sharp fragment of the broken glass.|Piece, Bit
8|Frail|Weak and delicate.|The frail old bird struggled to fly in the strong wind.|Weak, Delicate
8|Frantic|Wild or distraught with fear, anxiety, or other emotion.|The frantic mother searched everywhere for her lost keys.|Panicked, Anxious
8|Fraud|Wrongful or criminal deception intended to result in financial or personal gain.|The criminal was arrested for committing bank fraud.|Deception, Cheating
8|Fraudulent|Obtained, done by, or involving deception, especially criminal deception.|He used fraudulent methods to trick people out of their money.|Dishonest, Corrupt
8|Frequent|Occurring or done on many occasions, in many cases, or in quick succession.|She is a frequent visitor to the local library.|Regular, Repeated
8|Frightful|Very unpleasant, serious, or shocking.|A frightful storm damaged the roofs of several houses.|Terrible, Awful
8|Front|The side or part of an object that presents itself to view or that is normally seen or used first.|The teacher stood at the front of the classroom.|Fore, Face
8|Fund|A sum of money saved or made available for a particular purpose.|The school started a fund to buy new sports equipment.|Reserve, Pool
8|Fundamental|Forming a necessary base or core; of central importance.|Learning the alphabet is a fundamental part of reading.|Basic, Core
8|Galvanise|To shock or excite someone into taking action.|The inspiring speech will galvanise the team to play harder.|Spur, Stimulate
8|Garment|An item of clothing.|The king wore a luxurious velvet garment to the banquet.|Clothing, Apparel
8|Gauge|To estimate or determine the magnitude, amount, or volume of.|It is difficult to gauge exactly how deep the murky water is.|Measure, Estimate
8|Gilt|Covered thinly with gold leaf or gold paint.|The antique mirror was held in a beautiful gilt frame.|Gold-plated, Golden
8|Glib|Fluent and voluble but insincere and shallow.|No one trusted the salesman because of his glib and smooth talking.|Slick, Smooth
8|Gloomy|Dark or poorly lit, especially so as to appear depressing or frightening.|The haunted castle was damp, dark, and gloomy.|Dark, Dreary
9|Acumen|The ability to make good judgments and quick decisions.|Her brilliant business acumen helped her turn a tiny shop into a global empire.|Astuteness, Shrewdness, Sharpness
9|Bellicose|Demonstrating aggression and willingness to fight.|The bellicose general was always eager to start a new war.|Aggressive, Hostile, Warlike
9|Churlish|Rude in a mean-spirited and surly way.|It would be extremely churlish to complain about a gift that was given with love.|Rude, Ill-mannered, Surly

9|Didactic|Intended to teach, particularly in having moral instruction as an ulterior motive.|Aesop's fables are highly didactic stories meant to teach children right from wrong.|Educational, Instructive, Pedagogic
9|Enervate|To cause someone to feel drained of energy or vitality.|The blistering heat of the desert sun will rapidly enervate even the strongest explorer.|Exhaust, Tire, Drain
9|Fatuous|Silly and pointless.|He interrupted the serious meeting with a fatuous comment about his socks.|Silly, Foolish, Stupid
9|Gauche|Lacking ease or grace; unsophisticated and socially awkward.|It is considered gauche to chew with your mouth open at a formal dinner.|Awkward, Clumsy, Inelegant
9|Hegemony|Leadership or dominance, especially by one country or social group.|The Roman Empire maintained its military hegemony over Europe for centuries.|Dominance, Leadership, Supremacy
9|Incontrovertible|Not able to be denied or disputed.|The video footage provided incontrovertible proof that he stole the car.|Indisputable, Undeniable, Unquestionable
9|Lugubrious|Looking or sounding sad and dismal.|The bloodhound's drooping eyes gave it a permanently lugubrious expression.|Mournful, Gloomy, Sad
9|Misanthrope|A person who dislikes humankind and avoids human society.|The grumpy old misanthrope lived entirely alone and shouted at anyone who came near.|Cynic, Recluse, Hater
9|Nihilism|The rejection of all religious and moral principles.|His deep nihilism made him believe that nothing in life truly mattered.|Cynicism, Skepticism, Negativity
9|Obsequious|Obedient or attentive to an excessive or servile degree.|The obsequious waiter kept bowing and flattering the wealthy guests.|Servile, Fawning, Ingratiating
9|Pernicious|Having a harmful effect, especially in a gradual or subtle way.|The pernicious gossip slowly destroyed the politician's spotless reputation.|Harmful, Damaging, Destructive
9|Rambunctious|Uncontrollably exuberant; boisterous.|The rambunctious puppies knocked over the table and tore up the cushions.|Boisterous, Exuberant, Wild
9|Sanguine|Optimistic or positive, especially in an apparently bad or difficult situation.|Despite the terrible weather forecast, he remained sanguine about the picnic.|Optimistic, Hopeful, Positive
9|Trepidation|A feeling of fear or agitation about something that may happen.|She opened the letter containing her exam results with deep trepidation.|Fear, Apprehension, Dread
9|Unctuous|Excessively or ingratiatingly flattering; oily.|The salesman's unctuous manner made the customers feel quite uncomfortable.|Sycophantic, Fawning, Smarmy
9|Vacuous|Having or showing a lack of thought or intelligence; mindless.|He stared at the confusing puzzle with a totally vacuous expression.|Blank, Vacant, Empty
9|Winnow|To blow a current of air through grain in order to remove the chaff; to remove.|The judges had to winnow the thousands of entries down to just five finalists.|Sift, Filter, Separate
9|Xenophobe|A person having a dislike of or prejudice against people from other countries.|A true xenophobe fears foreign cultures and refuses to try new things.|Racist, Bigot, Chauvinist
9|Zealot|A person who is fanatical and uncompromising in pursuit of their ideals.|The religious zealot stood on the street corner preaching loudly to strangers.|Fanatic, Extremist, Radical
9|Abrogate|To repeal or do away with a law, right, or formal agreement.|The new president promised to abrogate the unpopular tax laws of his predecessor.|Repeal, Revoke, Overturn
9|Blandishment|A flattering or pleasing statement or action used to persuade someone.|She resisted all his blandishments and firmly refused to sell the house.|Flattery, Cajolery, Coaxing
9|Convivial|Friendly, lively, and enjoyable.|The pub had a wonderfully convivial atmosphere filled with laughter and music.|Friendly, Genial, Sociable
9|Glutton|An excessively greedy eater.|The glutton ate four huge slices of chocolate cake.|Gorger, Overeater
9|Grandeur|Splendor and impressiveness, especially of appearance or style.|The sheer grandeur of the royal palace amazed the visitors.|Splendor, Magnificence
9|Grate|To reduce something, especially food, to small shreds by rubbing it on a grater.|Please grate some cheese to sprinkle over the pasta.|Shred, Rub
9|Grotesque|Comically or repulsively ugly or distorted.|The gargoyle on the roof had a grotesque and frightening face.|Ugly, Deformed
9|Gung-ho|Unthinkingly enthusiastic and eager, especially about taking part in fighting or warfare.|The gung-ho soldiers cheered as they marched into battle.|Enthusiastic, Eager
9|Habitat|The natural home or environment of an animal, plant, or other organism.|The jungle is the natural habitat of the brightly colored parrot.|Home, Environment
9|Halt|To bring or come to an abrupt stop.|The train came to a sudden halt at the broken bridge.|Stop, Pause
9|Hamper|To hinder or impede the movement or progress of.|The heavy snow will hamper our efforts to drive to the city.|Hinder, Obstruct
9|Handsome|Good-looking.|The handsome prince rode his white horse through the town.|Attractive, Good-looking
9|Harass|To subject to aggressive pressure or intimidation.|The angry geese began to harass the people walking by the lake.|Pester, Bother
9|Heed|To pay attention to; take notice of.|You must heed the warning signs when swimming in the sea.|Notice, Listen
9|Helter-Skelter|In disorderly haste or confusion.|The children ran helter-skelter when the bell rang for break time.|Chaotic, Disorderly
9|Heroic|Having the characteristics of a hero or heroine; very brave.|The firefighter made a heroic dive to rescue the trapped cat.|Brave, Courageous
9|Hierarchical|Of the nature of a hierarchy; arranged in order of rank.|The army has a strict hierarchical structure from general to private.|Ranked, Ordered

9|Hierarchy|A system or organization in which people or groups are ranked one above the other according to status or authority.|The wolf pack has a clear hierarchy with an alpha male at the top.|Ranking, Order
9|Hindsight|Understanding of a situation or event only after it has happened or developed.|With hindsight, he realized he should have brought an umbrella.|Retrospect, Looking back
9|Hoax|A humorous or malicious deception.|The report of an alien spaceship landing turned out to be a massive hoax.|Trick, Prank
9|Holiday|An extended period of leisure and recreation, especially one spent away from home or in traveling.|We are going on holiday to the beach next week.|Vacation, Break
9|Holler|To give a loud shout or cry.|I heard him holler from the other side of the playground.|Shout, Yell
9|Humane|Having or showing compassion or benevolence.|The animal shelter treats all stray dogs in a kind and humane way.|Compassionate, Kind
9|Hypothetical|Based on or serving as a hypothesis.|Let's discuss a hypothetical situation where we could travel through time.|Theoretical, Imaginary
9|Identity|The fact of being who or what a person or thing is.|The superhero always keeps his true identity a secret.|Character, Name
9|Idle|Avoiding work; lazy.|The idle student refused to do any homework.|Lazy, Inactive
9|Idol|A person or thing that is greatly admired, loved, or revered.|The famous pop star is a major idol to millions of fans.|Hero, Icon
9|Idyllic|Extremely happy, peaceful, or picturesque.|The quiet countryside cottage was the perfect idyllic retreat.|Perfect, Peaceful
10|Adumbrate|To report or represent in outline; indicate faintly.|The architect used a quick sketch to adumbrate his grand vision for the new cathedral.|Outline, Sketch, Foreshadow
10|Bifurcate|To divide into two branches or forks.|At the ancient oak tree, the muddy trail will bifurcate into two separate paths.|Divide, Fork, Split
10|Cogent|Clear, logical, and convincing.|The lawyer presented a highly cogent argument that easily won over the jury.|Convincing, Compelling, Persuasive
10|Desiccate|To remove the moisture from something; cause to become completely dry.|The intense desert heat will quickly desiccate any plants left without water.|Dry, Dehydrate, Parch
10|Evanescent|Soon passing out of sight, memory, or existence; quickly fading.|The beautiful rainbow was evanescent, disappearing entirely after just a few minutes.|Vanishing, Fading, Fleeting
10|Fulsome|Complimentary or flattering to an excessive degree.|The mayor gave a fulsome speech praising the hero, bordering on being insincere.|Excessive, Extravagant, Overdone
10|Garrulity|Excessive talkativeness, especially on trivial matters.|Her annoying garrulity meant that no one else could get a word in during the meeting.|Loquacity, Verbosity, Talkativeness
10|Heterogeneous|Diverse in character or content.|London's population is highly heterogeneous, with people from all over the world.|Diverse, Varied, Mixed

10|Intransigent|Unwilling or refusing to change one's views or to agree about something.|The intransigent union leaders completely refused to negotiate a compromise.|Uncompromising, Stubborn, Inflexible

10|Juxtaposition|The fact of two things being seen or placed close together with contrasting effect.|The juxtaposition of the shiny glass skyscraper next to the medieval castle was striking.|Comparison, Contrast, Proximity
10|Laconic|Using very few words.|The tough detective gave a laconic reply of simply 'No'.|Brief, Concise, Terse
10|Mellifluous|Sweet or musical; pleasant to hear.|The opera singer had a rich, mellifluous voice that enchanted the whole audience.|Sweet-sounding, Musical, Harmonious
10|Neophyte|A person who is new to a subject, skill, or belief.|Because he was a neophyte at coding, he asked a lot of basic questions.|Beginner, Novice, Rookie
10|Obdurate|Stubbornly refusing to change one's opinion or course of action.|Despite her friends begging her, the obdurate girl refused to apologize.|Stubborn, Inflexible, Unyielding
10|Perspicacious|Having a ready insight into and understanding of things.|The perspicacious detective quickly realized that the 'victim' was actually the criminal.|Astute, Shrewd, Perceptive
10|Quixotic|Exceedingly idealistic; unrealistic and impractical.|His quixotic plan to end world hunger by handing out free cakes was never going to work.|Idealistic, Unrealistic, Utopian
10|Recondite|Little known; abstruse.|The professor loved to lecture on recondite topics that confused his students.|Obscure, Abstruse, Arcane
10|Sagacious|Having or showing keen mental discernment and good judgment.|The sagacious old owl gave excellent advice to the animals of the forest.|Wise, Clever, Intelligent
10|Trenchant|Vigorous or incisive in expression or style.|The critic wrote a trenchant review that harshly exposed the flaws in the movie.|Incisive, Cutting, Pointed
10|Umbrage|Offense or annoyance.|He took extreme umbrage at her suggestion that his work was messy.|Offense, Resentment, Indignation
10|Vicissitude|A change of circumstances or fortune, typically one that is unwelcome.|The ancient castle survived wars and storms, enduring every vicissitude of time.|Change, Alteration, Fluctuation
10|Welter|A large number of items in no order; a confused mass.|His messy desk was a welter of paperwork, coffee cups, and broken pens.|Jumble, Tangle, Clutter

10|Xenophobia|Intense or irrational dislike or fear of people from other countries.|The politician's speech was dangerous because it encouraged xenophobia among the crowd.|Prejudice, Bigotry, Nationalism
10|Yoke|To attach two things together; or a burden of oppression.|The cruel dictator forced his people to bear the heavy yoke of taxation.|Burden, Oppression, Bondage
10|Zeugma|A figure of speech in which a word applies to two others in different senses.|'He caught a cold and the last train' is a perfect example of a zeugma.|Pun, Wordplay, Figure of speech
10|Immaculate|Perfectly clean, neat, or tidy.|She kept her bedroom immaculate, without a speck of dust anywhere.|Spotless, Perfect
10|Immerse|To dip or submerge in a liquid.|You need to immerse the tea bag in boiling water.|Submerge, Dip
10|Impassive|Not feeling or showing emotion.|The royal guard maintained a completely impassive expression.|Expressionless, Blank
10|Impede|To delay or prevent someone or something by obstructing them; hinder.|The fallen tree will impede our journey through the forest.|Hinder, Obstruct
10|Imperative|Of vital importance; crucial.|It is imperative that you wear a helmet while riding your bike.|Crucial, Vital
10|Inadequate|Lacking the quality or quantity required; insufficient.|The meager rations were inadequate for the hungry soldiers.|Insufficient, Poor
10|Incision|A surgical cut made in skin or flesh.|The surgeon made a small, precise incision during the operation.|Cut, Slice
10|Incline|To feel willing or favorably disposed toward an action, belief, or attitude.|I incline to believe her story because she is very honest.|Tend, Lean
10|Inconsequential|Not important or significant.|The minor scratch on the car's bumper was completely inconsequential.|Insignificant, Unimportant
10|Incumbent|Necessary for someone as a duty or responsibility.|It is incumbent on all of us to protect the environment.|Binding, Obligatory
10|Inequity|Lack of fairness or justice.|The protest was against the profound inequity in the new laws.|Unfairness, Injustice
10|Inert|Lacking the ability or strength to move.|The injured animal lay inert on the side of the road.|Motionless, Still
10|Infantile|Of or occurring among babies or very young children.|His infantile behavior and tantrums annoyed his older sister.|Childish, Immature
10|Inferior|Lower in rank, status, or quality.|The cheap plastic toy was of vastly inferior quality to the wooden one.|Lesser, Substandard
10|Inhale|To breathe in air, smoke, or gas.|Take a deep breath and inhale the fresh mountain air.|Breathe in, Gasp
10|Innocuous|Not harmful or offensive.|Despite its scary appearance, the spider is completely innocuous.|Harmless, Safe
10|Insane|In a state of mind which prevents normal perception, behavior, or social interaction; seriously mentally ill.|Driving a car at 150 mph on a rainy night is an insane idea.|Crazy, Mad
10|Insinuate|To suggest or hint something bad or reprehensible in an indirect and unpleasant way.|Are you trying to insinuate that I stole the missing money?|Hint, Suggest
10|Intangible|Unable to be touched or grasped; not having physical presence.|The wind is an intangible force that you can feel but not hold.|Invisible, Impalpable
10|Interfere|To take part or intervene in an activity without invitation or necessity.|Please do not interfere while I am trying to fix the computer.|Meddle, Intrude
10|Interrupt|To stop the continuous progress of an activity or process.|Please do not interrupt the teacher while she is speaking.|Stop, Break
10|Inundate|To overwhelm someone with things or people to be dealt with.|The angry customers will inundate the shop with complaints.|Overwhelm, Flood
10|Ironic|Using or characterized by irony.|It is highly ironic that the fire station burned to the ground.|Sarcastic, Paradoxical
10|Isolation|The process or fact of isolating or being isolated.|The hermit lived in total isolation in his cabin in the woods.|Separation, Solitude
10|Jargon|Special words or expressions that are used by a particular profession or group.|The contract was full of confusing legal jargon that no one understood.|Slang, Terminology
